title: Pulling and attaching a thrown spout
subtitle: Shaping a pulled clay spout and joining it to a thrown jug body
excerpt: A pulled spout for a wheel-thrown jug is formed by pulling a thick rope of clay into a tapered tube and shaping it to a natural curve. It is scored, slipped, and pressed onto the jug body at the leather-hard stage, then smoothed at the join.
sourceNotes: Spout pulling and attachment technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A clay drying stage where the piece is firm enough to handle and join without distorting, but still slightly damp for adhesion.
The standard method for joining two pieces of clay: scratch the contact surfaces with a needle tool to roughen them (score), brush on liquid clay (slip) as a glue, then press the pieces together.
Shaping a form by drawing clay upward and outward between wet hands. Used for both thrown walls and for forming handles and spouts off the wheel.

content.text: A
pulled

spout is made from a thick rope of clay worked between wet hands until it tapers to a natural tube. The spout opening is left round at the tip and trimmed to the desired angle after pulling. Attachment uses the standard
score-and-slip
method when both spout and jug body are at the
leather-hard
stage.

attrs.content: Wear an N95 mask when trimming leather-hard stoneware. Trimming and scoring generate fine silica-bearing dust. Fire kilns in a well-ventilated space.

items.name: Liquid slip (same clay body thinned with water)
Thrown jug body at leather-hard stage

content.text: Pulling the spout
Form 80 g of well-conditioned stoneware into a thick rope 40 mm in diameter and 80 mm long. Hold one end in the left hand.
Wet the right hand and draw downward along the rope with index finger and thumb lightly squeezed, thinning and elongating the clay. Keep both hands wet throughout.
After each downward pass, rotate the rope 90 degrees and pull again. Continue until the rope is 100-120 mm long and tapers from 30 mm at the base to 15 mm at the tip.
Curve the pulled spout slightly by bending it gently with both thumbs. Set it on a bat to stiffen slightly before cutting to length.
Preparing the spout opening

When the spout is firm enough to hold its shape but still slightly damp, cut the base end at the angle it will meet the jug body, usually 30-45 degrees to the spout axis. Cut the tip at the desired pouring angle. Trim both cuts clean with the fettling knife.
Attaching to the jug
Hold the spout against the jug body at the desired position and mark the footprint of the base opening with the needle tool.

Cut a neat hole through the jug wall within the marked footprint using the needle tool and fettling knife. The hole allows the liquid to flow into the spout; size it to match the inner diameter of the spout base.
Score both the outer edge of the jug hole and the base end of the spout thoroughly with the needle tool. Brush slip onto both scored surfaces.
Press the spout firmly onto the jug wall, aligning the openings. Smooth the join inside and out with a fingertip and the wooden rib.
Drying and firing

Cover the jug loosely with plastic for 24 hours to allow the spout join to dry slowly and evenly at the same rate as the body. Rapid drying at the join point risks cracking. Fire to bisque temperature before glazing.
Variations
Make the spout hole smaller and pierce it with multiple fine holes using the needle tool to create a built-in strainer for loose-leaf tea.
Use the same pulling technique to make a handle before spout attachment; both pulled in the same clay weight and session.

items.symptom: Spout cracks at the join after drying
items.cause: Spout and body at different moisture levels when joined; differential shrinkage cracked the join
items.fix: Join spout and body at the same leather-hard stage and cover the whole piece slowly for 24 hours

items.cause: Spout clay too soft when attached
items.fix: Allow the pulled spout to stiffen on the bat until it can hold its shape unassisted before attaching
